title: Slip Trailing: Dots and Lines on Air-Dry Clay
subtitle: Pipe clay slip to build raised dot and line patterns on a flat slab
excerpt: Use a slip-trailing bottle or small piping bag to pipe clay slip onto a flat slab in dots, lines, and loops. Allow the trails to stiffen before handling. A classic, no-kiln decorating technique that creates tactile, raised surface patterns.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from traditional slip-trailing methods adapted for air-dry clay without kiln firing.

glossaryTerms.definition: A decorating technique where liquid clay slip is piped onto a clay surface through a nozzle to create raised lines, dots, or patterns
Liquid clay thinned to a piping consistency; it bonds to the clay surface as both dry and creates a raised decoration
The stage at which clay holds its shape and no longer sticks to your hands, but still contains enough moisture to bond with applied slip
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used here as the surface to decorate

content.text: Slip trailing
is the pottery equivalent of icing a cake. A slip-trailing bottle (a small squeeze bottle with a fine nozzle) or a small piping bag is filled with clay
slip
and squeezed onto the surface of a flat
slab

to build up raised dots, lines, and loops. The trails dry proud of the surface, giving a tactile, dimensional quality. No kiln required. This technique works on any flat or gently curved air-dry clay piece, including tiles, bowls, and dishes.

materials.name: Slip-trailing bottle with 2 mm nozzle, or small piping bag with fine nozzle

content.text: Preparation

Prepare the trailing slip by thinning white slip with water to a consistency slightly thicker than single cream. It should flow steadily from a spoon without dripping too fast. Too thick and it will not flow through the nozzle; too thin and the trails collapse flat.

Fill the trailing bottle or piping bag no more than two-thirds full. Squeeze a test trail onto a spare piece of baking paper. The trail should hold a slight ridge when it lands. Adjust the consistency if needed.
Sketch your dot and line pattern on paper first. Simple repeating patterns -- rows of dots, a grid of crosses, a border of loops -- look strong. Avoid very complex designs for a first attempt.
Preparing the slab
Roll the clay on baking paper using 6 mm guide sticks to produce an even
slab
6 mm thick. Cut to a tile shape approximately 15 x 15 cm.
Allow the slab to rest for 30-60 minutes until it is at
leather-hard
stage. Applying slip to a very soft slab causes the slab to bow under the weight of the trails. A leather-hard surface also bonds with the
slip
trails more securely.
Trailing the pattern
Hold the nozzle of the
slip-trailing

bottle about 5 mm above the clay surface. Squeeze gently and steadily as you move your hand to create a line, or pause and squeeze briefly to create a dot. Let the slip land on the clay; do not drag the nozzle through it.
Work the pattern systematically from one side of the tile to the other to avoid resting your hand on completed trails.
For dots: squeeze a small amount of slip, release pressure, then lift the nozzle cleanly. A small peak will form; it flattens slightly as the slip settles.
For lines: squeeze steadily and move in one smooth motion. Trailing too slowly produces a thick, wobbly line; too fast and it stretches thin and breaks.
Allow the completed pattern to rest undisturbed until the slip trails are firm and no longer shiny -- usually 1-2 hours. Do not move the tile during this time.
What success looks like
When dry, each
slip

trail should stand slightly proud of the slab surface as a raised, rounded ridge. Dots should be hemispherical. Lines should have even width throughout, with clean start and end points. The trails should feel firmly attached and not lift if you press them very lightly with a fingertip. The contrast between the white trail and the clay body should be clear.
Common mistakes
Slip too thin: trails flatten to nothing and blend into the surface. Add more slip powder or clay to thicken.
Dragging the nozzle through the slip: smears the trail sideways. Always trail above the surface and let gravity land the slip.
Moving the tile before the slip has stiffened: trails slide and the pattern is lost. Leave completely undisturbed for at least 1 hour.
Applying trails to a very soft slab: the slab bows and trails crack when it flattens. Always wait for leather-hard stage.

items.problem: Trails cracked or separated from the slab when drying
items.cause: Slip and slab dried at different rates or slip was applied to bone-dry clay
items.fix: Apply trails only to leather-hard clay. Cover loosely with a plastic bag and slow the drying over 24 hours.

items.problem: Lines have uneven width with thick and thin sections
items.fix: Practice on baking paper before starting the tile. Move your hand at a steady pace and keep pressure even throughout each stroke.

items.problem: The trailing bottle nozzle keeps blocking
items.cause: Slip is too thick or has dried in the nozzle
items.fix: Thin the slip slightly with water and cap the nozzle between uses. Clear blockages by briefly soaking the nozzle in water.

items.problem: Raised trails snapped off when the piece was handled
items.cause: The slab was moved while trails were still wet
items.fix: Allow 2 full hours before any movement. Handle by lifting from the base rather than the sides.
